author: @dvhx
text: It is also possible to burn larger parts of steel, like a wire or strip of sheet metal - if the steel is amorphous. Steel doesn't really want to be in amorphous state, but it can be forced by squirting molten steel onto a cooled copper cylinder. By quickly cooling it it will stay in amorphous state and when you light it with a match it will burn. I learned this in "Modern magnetically soft materials" at university. Read "Splat quenching" on wikipedia. These materials have some interesting magnetic properties.

author: @douglascalhoun6471
text: The first time I learned that tool steel would burn was around 1997. I was grinding O1 steel on a surface grinder when the debris that wasn't being sucked into the dust collector ignited. Quite the revelation while learning how to be a machinist in a tool and die shop. Very valuable lesson for people to consider. Keep up the good work.
